doom.driver: Russia's neighbor to scrap ban on nuclear weapons, says 'situation is getting worse'.
doom.blurb: ANOTHER DOMINO DROPS ON NUCLEAR NORMS
doom.summary: First Finland, now another Nordic/Baltic neighbor is ditching its nuclear weapons ban — because apparently watching Russia level Kyiv in its 'most massive' attack yet (again) has a way of changing your defense posture. This is the slow-motion collapse of post-Cold War nuclear taboo in real time, and the situation is, per the headline's own admission, 'getting worse.' Meanwhile Ebola treatments are being trialed in DRC and Africa CDC just confirmed a Marburg case in Uganda, which is the kind of biotech sidebar that would dominate news on any other week.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: Russia continues grinding missile and drone campaigns on Kyiv while Ukraine's asymmetric long-range strikes begin rattling Moscow's confidence — no resolution in sight.

factors.name: Nuclear
factors.detail: A second European nation moves to scrap its nuclear weapons ban in weeks, signaling accelerating erosion of post-Cold War non-proliferation norms under Russian pressure.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: Trump's crypto conflicts of interest and OpenAI's 5% US government stake talks suggest institutional guardrails on AI and finance are bending in new directions.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: Kimi K2.7 rolls into GitHub Copilot and AI operational tools proliferate, but nothing today crosses a meaningful capability threshold.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Simultaneous Ebola outbreak (438 deaths in DRC) and a confirmed Marburg case in Uganda is an underreported dual-pathogen situation worth watching.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing — night scene aftermath, all light from artificial sources, deep navy and smoldering orange palette, camera positioned at street level in Vilnius Old Town looking down a narrow cobblestone lane toward the neoclassical Presidential Palace, its white stone facade lit harshly by a single security floodlight casting long black shadows across iron railings, the Vytis coat of arms — white armored knight on red — mounted above the palace gate and caught in the beam, a lone municipal worker in a reflective vest pushing a wide broom across ancient cobblestones strewn with scattered papers and press litter from the day's announcement, a forest of abandoned broadcast microphone stands left on the palace steps like bare winter saplings, one overturned, cables coiling across the stone like dark veins, a crumpled official document face-down on the wet cobbles catching smoldering orange light from a single iron streetlamp above, Gothic church spires of the Old Town rising black against deep navy sky behind the palace, a military jeep idling at the far end of the lane its red tail-lights bleeding into the mist, the flat forested Baltic horizon barely visible beyond rooftops, the whole scene pressing with cold northern European damp and the heavy silence of a decision that cannot be undone.

dawn.driver: Nasa launches mission to save falling space telescope. A Nasa-funded robot has blasted off to catch a falling telescope in mid-orbit and blast it back to safety before it burns up.
dawn.blurb: HUMANITY SENDS A ROBOT TO CATCH A FALLING STAR
dawn.summary: Against a backdrop of war, heat, and geopolitical fracture, NASA quietly pulled off something that would have been science fiction a generation ago: launching a robotic spacecraft to intercept a tumbling telescope in orbit and haul it back from the brink of destruction. It's a reminder that human ingenuity doesn't pause for geopolitics — we are, right now, performing orbital rescue missions. The same civilization that struggles to keep peace on the ground is also sending robots to catch telescopes mid-fall, preserving our eyes on the universe.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: A robotic orbital rescue mission demonstrates real-time mastery of space mechanics that would have seemed impossible decades ago.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: The Venezuelan security guard pulled alive after eight days in rubble is a visceral testament to survival and the will to rescue strangers.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: Preserving scientific instruments like space telescopes keeps humanity's long-term climate and planetary monitoring capabilities intact.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: Oregon ER doctors successfully fought a national staffing firm under a new state law, showing local solidarity can win systemic battles.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Ebola treatment trials beginning in DR Congo signal that even in active outbreak zones, the global medical community is showing up with tools and resolve.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Ground-level view looking straight up from below, camera pressed flat against a polished concrete floor, gazing up at the underside of a standing figure — a young woman in a dark hoodie, sneakers planted wide apart, head tilted back, neck craned upward toward a ceiling-mounted monitor screen casting cold blue-white light down across her upturned face and jaw. The environment is a cramped Flagstaff mission control room seen from below — cable trays and ventilation ducts crossing the ceiling above her like a steel canopy, amber light bleeding in warm pools from desk lamps at the edges of frame, the contrast between cold monitor glow above and warm desk amber below splitting the scene in two registers. Her arms are crossed tight across her chest, knuckles white, one hand gripping the opposite sleeve — posture of held breath, of someone watching something they cannot touch. On the screen overhead, a ghostly blue-white pixel readout glows with orbital trajectory data, abstract curves suggesting something arcing downward then leveling. Deep indigo dominates the upper frame, the monitor's electric blue-white slicing through it, her face caught in that cold light — golden amber warming her shoulders from below, teal shadows pooling in the corners of the room above her head.
